In the original Death Wish trilogy, Paul Kersey was a Commercial  Architect. 
Wonder why they changed his profession?
@Phil PJ Davis: It wasn't a trilogy.  They've even played the 4th on TV multiple times.

They also changed the setting to Chicago from New York, which makes sense given how cleaned up NYC became and the ongoing gun violence in Chicago. In the original, the police sent him to Chicago at the end, and I wouldn't be surprised if they reverse that and send him to NYC in this (also since apparently they filmed in Montreal at the end, which is frequently used as a stand-in for NYC along with Toronto for cheaper costs)....I can see how changing the profession works for this, the character is supposed to have some civic connection that gets upended after his family is attacked. It didn't have to change, but architect was fitting to the urban development in the gritty 70s, doctor is more polished and upscale for today, and even more dramatic departure for him since he saves and helps people.

This could be an interesting remake, but it is going to lack the dark, moody atmosphere of the original. Charles Bronson's performance is subdued, but menacing.  He epitomizes an ordinary person pushed to the edge and decides he would do something about it.  He's believable as Mr. everyperson learning to shoot a gun and putting himself in situations where he would be mugged or attacked so he can get his revenge on all low-life criminals.  For its time, the movie created talk of whether it is moral or immoral for a person to take the law in his own hands.  And Herbie Hancock's jazzy score is an additional highlight.  This new remake looks more like an action movie, like a cross between Die Hard and Taken.

I agree. In the 70s and 80s citys had a certain gritty and dirty look, and with the extremely violent scenes it made the first 2 death wishs a masterpiece. This remake looks too clean and like a typical action movie. I am glad they remade it tho, i will enjoy it no matter what

Well justice and due process involve a "fair trial". If it looked like you might be in trouble for doing something wrong should someone just shoot you on the spot? What if it was for stealing a soda? Do you deserve to die for that? What if you actually didn't do anything at all? Do you deserve to die for that?

Justice is blind, and for that reason everyone deserves to be treated fairly.

I'm talking about cartels and gangs here, not petty theft. Everyone knows where they are they just don't want to get involved out fear, which is understandable. However if say a group of paid ex-military began taking out the gang problem from the shadows problem solved. You are right justice can be blind, but sometimes afraid to wet the sword in a hypocritical world of where doing nothing just helps evil triumph.

More like a Death Wish remake lol

A better DEATH WISH movie would have recontextualized the original story around current events by portraying Kersey as a pacifistic, liberal white man turned cold vigilante killer of mostly black criminals. While Kersey privately considers his actions to be of a MORAL character, show BLM protesters frame his actions as inherently RACIAL in character. Portray the national media as being  disinterested in the killings of black gang members and/or aiding the protesters. Have a police commissioner genuinely interested in arresting Kersey [while building an inaccurate suspect profile] being occasionally obstructed by a police force that mostly identifies with the killer. Make the movie about collective cognitive dissonance and competing narratives struggling to define the social and historical nature of current events [Kersey's fictionalized vigilantism] with each side falsely caricaturing what they each oppose and have projected onto it.

It would make the city streets of Detroit feel increasingly claustrophobic leading to a Wild Bunch standoff/chase sequence between Kersey, street gangs, protesters, and police in the climax.

Instead, we're getting generic action escapism that will leave your mind the second you walk out of the theatre.

This take is incredibly pandering in its portrayal of Kersey as a white savior for victimized blacks against either white villains, other non-black minority villains, or from blacks themselves. It even puts Kersey in a grey hoody like Trayvon Martin to emphasize Kersey's identification with the common man/downtrodden minorities. It is cowardly because it sheepishly avoids all of the social commentary that could have elevated the material, imbuing it with new meaning, subtext, etc.
